title: Keyboard and accessibility-tree audit

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific HTML: Semantic and Accessible Web Documents draft for Keyboard and accessibility-tree audit; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: Web Content Accessibility Guidelines 2.2; W3C Recommendation 2023; provisions applicable to Keyboard and accessibility-tree audit
sources.claim: An HTML accessibility audit combines DOM and accessibility-tree inspection, keyboard use, names and states, zoom, content review, and automated checks.

sources.locator: WAI-ARIA Authoring Practices Guide; living guide accessed 2026-07; provisions applicable to Keyboard and accessibility-tree audit
sources.claim: An HTML accessibility audit combines DOM and accessibility-tree inspection, keyboard use, names and states, zoom, content review, and automated checks.

canonicalExplanation.summary: An HTML accessibility audit combines DOM and accessibility-tree inspection, keyboard use, names and states, zoom, content review, and automated checks.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A learner completes a form using only keyboard, inspects labels and errors, then uses an automated scanner to find additional issues. Your goal is to record and repair findings from a manual keyboard and accessibility-tree review. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Document foundations; Text and page semantics; Forms and native controls; Native semantics before ARIA; Keyboard navigation and skip links. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: An HTML accessibility audit combines DOM and accessibility-tree inspection, keyboard use, names and states, zoom, content review, and automated checks. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Automated tools detect only a subset, passing rules is not proof, and screen-reader testing needs task-focused interpretation. This draft therefore rejects: A zero-error automated accessibility report certifies that the page is accessible.

includes: Inspect headings, landmarks, names, roles, states, focus order and browser zoom behavior.
Record and repair findings from a manual keyboard and accessibility-tree review.
excludes: Automated tools detect only a subset, passing rules is not proof, and screen-reader testing needs task-focused interpretation.

outcomes: Record and repair findings from a manual keyboard and accessibility-tree review.

examples.title: Keyboard and accessibility-tree audit: concrete case
examples.situation: A learner completes a form using only keyboard, inspects labels and errors, then uses an automated scanner to find additional issues.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: A learner completes a form using only keyboard, inspects labels and errors, then uses an automated scanner to find additional issues.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Treat automation as bounded evidence and combine it with keyboard, structure, name/state, visual, and task-based human checks.
examples.result: The result follows because Treat automation as bounded evidence and combine it with keyboard, structure, name/state, visual, and task-based human checks.

examples.title: Keyboard and accessibility-tree audit: boundary case
examples.situation: Automated tools detect only a subset, passing rules is not proof, and screen-reader testing needs task-focused interpretation.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Start from the documented boundary: Automated tools detect only a subset, passing rules is not proof, and screen-reader testing needs task-focused interpretation.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Reject the misconception: A zero-error automated accessibility report certifies that the page is accessible.
examples.result: The boundary requires this correction: Treat automation as bounded evidence and combine it with keyboard, structure, name/state, visual, and task-based human checks.

analogy.example: A labeled document outline can model Keyboard and accessibility-tree audit, with structure carrying meaning before decoration.

limitations: Documents cannot model parser recovery, browser APIs, or interactive semantics exactly.

recap.summary: You should now be able to record and repair findings from a manual keyboard and accessibility-tree review. Keep this boundary in view: Automated tools detect only a subset, passing rules is not proof, and screen-reader testing needs task-focused interpretation.
